job_source.ad: Job posting on Indeed.com for "Line Service Technician - General Aviation" at Rust's Flying Service

employment_history___most_recent_or_curr.last_job_title: Senior Line Service Technician / Ramp Lead
employment_history___most_recent_or_curr.describe_your_job_duties:
In my current role as Senior Line Service Technician / Ramp Lead at Rust's Flying Service, I am responsible for overseeing day-to-day ramp operations in support of the company’s floatplane and wheel aircraft sightseeing and charter flights. My duties are both hands-on and supervisory.

On a typical day I arrive about an hour before the first scheduled departure to open up the ramp, perform a quick walk-through of all assigned aircraft parking areas, and check fuel trucks, tugs, and ground support equipment for readiness (fluid levels, tire pressure, general condition). I coordinate closely with the dispatch office and the pilots to review the flight schedule, anticipated fuel loads, passenger counts, and any special requirements such as cargo, fishing gear, or lodge resupply flights.

My core responsibilities include:
- Aircraft servicing: fueling single- and twin-engine aircraft (primarily De Havilland Beavers and Otters, Cessna 206s, and 208 Caravans), topping off oil, checking and replenishing other consumables, and assisting pilots with pre-flight inspections as needed. I am trained on both Avgas and Jet-A operations and strictly follow company fueling procedures, including bonding/grounding, use of fuel sample jars, and documenting fuel uplifts.
- Ramp safety and marshalling: safely marshalling aircraft on and off the dock and ramp using standardized hand signals, chocks, and tie-downs. During busy summer months, I manage a small team to ensure that aircraft movements are sequenced efficiently and that pedestrian traffic (passengers and visitors) is kept clear of propellers, float struts, and loading areas. I am responsible for enforcing the use of high-visibility vests and hearing protection on the ramp.
- Passenger handling support: greeting guests as they arrive, helping them with luggage and outdoor gear, fitting and briefing them on life vests and basic safety around floatplanes, and guiding them to and from aircraft in coordination with pilots and customer service. During peak season, I frequently help answer common questions about flight times, weather delays, and what to expect on flightseeing and bear-viewing tours, always trying to keep nervous first-time flyers comfortable and informed.
- Cargo and weight-and-balance support: loading and securing cargo such as coolers, camping equipment, fishing rods, and lodge supplies in a way that respects aircraft weight-and-balance limits and center-of-gravity considerations. I carefully follow pilots’ instructions, weigh luggage when required, and keep detailed notes on what is loaded into each aircraft compartment.
- Equipment operation and care: operating tugs, tow bars, forklifts, and ground power units (GPUs) to move aircraft and support maintenance crews. I perform basic preventive maintenance checks on ground equipment (fuel filters, hose inspections, battery checks) and promptly report defects or irregularities to maintenance.
- Environmental and spill response: ensuring that all fueling and oil handling follows environmental regulations and company policies. I maintain spill kits at fuel points, train newer staff on their use, and have personally responded to a minor fuel spill, containing and cleaning it up according to our written procedures and then documenting the incident.
- Training and leadership: as Ramp Lead during many shifts, I help train new line service technicians on marshalling signals, safe fueling techniques, floatplane dock operations, and customer interaction. I create and adjust daily task lists, rotate staff through positions to avoid fatigue, and step in directly when situations get busy or complex. I’ve helped refine our seasonal training checklist and have informally mentored several younger coworkers who were new to aviation.
- Seasonal and weather-related tasks: in shoulder seasons and on colder days, I assist with engine preheating, wing covers, and other cold-weather procedures. When weather delays flights, I help coordinate new departure times with dispatch, secure aircraft, and assist front desk staff with keeping passengers updated.

Overall, my job has required a high level of attention to detail, situational awareness around spinning propellers and moving aircraft, and strong communication with pilots, dispatchers, mechanics, and passengers. I have consistently been one of the go-to people for resolving last-minute ramp issues, troubleshooting fueling questions, and stepping in where needed to keep operations safe and on time.
employment_history___most_recent_or_curr.reason_for_leaving: I am seeking internal advancement into a more permanent, year-round position with expanded responsibilities in operations and safety, rather than remaining solely in a seasonal ramp role. After several years on the line service team, I feel I have reached a point where I either need to move into a role with more decision-making authority and long-term growth (such as operations coordinator or assistant base manager) or risk stagnating in my current position. I have communicated this to my supervisor and made it clear that I value the company and would prefer to stay within Rust's Flying Service if there is an opportunity to transition into a role that leverages my operational experience, knowledge of our routes and aircraft, and interest in safety management and training. If such a role is not available in the near term, I am open to exploring other aviation employers in the Anchorage area that offer more defined career paths.

employment_history___second_employer.describe_your_job_duties_second_employme:
At Anchorage Airfield Services, I worked as a Line Service Technician supporting a mix of general aviation piston aircraft, small corporate jets, and air taxi operators at Merrill Field. The job gave me my foundational experience in ramp operations and safety.

My main responsibilities included:
- Fueling and servicing aircraft: I fueled single- and twin-engine piston aircraft with 100LL Avgas and occasionally handled Jet-A fueling for turboprops and light business jets. This involved positioning fuel trucks, properly grounding the aircraft and truck, using ladders safely around wings and fuselages, and following strict procedures for topping off tanks and visually checking fuel caps and vents. I also checked and added engine oil when requested by pilots and cleaned windscreens and leading edges as part of turnarounds.
- Aircraft movement and parking: I marshalled aircraft into and out of parking spots and hangars, using standard marshalling signals and radios when required. I regularly towed aircraft using tugs and tow bars, ensuring that nose gear locks were in place and that propeller clearance was maintained. During winter months, I helped with pushing aircraft in and out of hangars to minimize exposure to snow and ice.
- Customer service: I assisted transient pilots and passengers with baggage handling, arranging courtesy cars or taxis, and answering basic questions about local services, weather briefings, and airport procedures (within the limits of my role, always directing them to official sources for weather and flight planning). I often worked the front line during busy weekends, greeting arriving aircraft on the ramp and coordinating with the front desk staff.
- Ramp and facility upkeep: I was responsible for keeping the ramp area clean and free of FOD (Foreign Object Debris), organizing chocks, cones, and tie-down ropes, and performing basic housekeeping tasks in the hangar and fuel farm areas. I conducted regular FOD walks, especially after windy days or heavy traffic, and reported any ramp surface damage or hazards to my supervisor.
- Documentation and safety: I logged all fuel transactions accurately, noted tail numbers, quantities, and fuel grades, and obtained signatures from pilots when required. I participated in monthly safety briefings covering topics such as ramp incidents, near-misses, and seasonal hazards (e.g., icing, wildlife on the field). I also completed in-house training on hazardous materials handling, spill prevention, and emergency response.

Working at Anchorage Airfield Services taught me the importance of clear communication with pilots, attention to detail with fuel types and quantities, and the need to constantly scan the environment for potential safety issues. It also gave me experience working in varying weather conditions, from summer rain to winter snow and ice, and how to adapt ramp procedures accordingly.
employment_history___second_employer.reason_for_leaving_second_employment: I left Anchorage Airfield Services to take a position with Rust's Flying Service that offered more exposure to floatplane operations, a higher volume of seasonal tourist flights, and a clearer path to leadership responsibilities on the ramp. Rust's also provided a modest pay increase and the opportunity to work with a well-known local operator whose focus on Alaska flightseeing and lodge support aligned more closely with my long-term goal of building a career in bush and backcountry aviation support.

employment_history___third_employer.address_third_employment: 845 Eagle River Loop Road, Eagle River, AK 99577
employment_history___third_employer.describe_your_job_duties_third_employmen:
At Northern Trails Outdoor Outfitters, a regional outdoor gear and clothing store, I worked in a combined role that covered both front-of-house retail sales and back-of-house warehouse and inventory support. This job helped me develop strong customer service skills and a solid work ethic before transitioning into aviation.

My primary duties included:
- Customer service and sales: assisting customers in selecting appropriate outdoor gear for hiking, camping, fishing, and light mountaineering in Alaska’s conditions. I spent a lot of time asking questions about where they were going, what time of year, and their experience level, then recommending appropriate boots, layers, rain gear, and safety equipment. I learned the product lines well enough to explain differences between entry-level and technical gear and to help people stay within their budget without sacrificing safety.
- Point-of-sale operations: operating the cash register, processing credit card and cash transactions, handling returns and exchanges in line with store policy, and balancing my till at the end of shifts. I was trusted to open and close the store on some weekends, including counting down drawers, preparing bank deposits for the manager, and securing the premises.
- Inventory and stocking: receiving shipments in the back warehouse, checking packing lists against purchase orders, reporting discrepancies, and organizing merchandise on shelves and racks. I helped with regular inventory counts and worked with the store manager to identify slow-moving items that could be featured in promotions or moved to clearance.
- Merchandising and displays: setting up seasonal product displays (e.g., spring hiking, summer fishing, winter layering), arranging mannequins, and ensuring signage and pricing were accurate. I enjoyed the creative aspect of making displays that told a story—for example, building a mock campsite in the front of the store with a tent, sleeping bags, camp stove, and bear canister to spark conversations with customers.
- Safety and basic equipment knowledge: I developed a working understanding of essential outdoor safety topics such as hypothermia prevention, bear awareness, and basic trip planning. While I always made it clear that I was not a guide or instructor, I shared my own weekend hiking experiences and directed customers to reputable resources and classes.

This job was not directly in aviation, but it gave me extensive experience dealing with the public, handling busy seasonal rushes (especially before fishing and hunting seasons), staying organized, and working as part of a small, tight-knit team. Those skills transferred well when I moved into customer-facing aviation support roles where clear communication, patience, and reliability are crucial.
employment_history___third_employer.reason_for_leaving_third_employment: I left Northern Trails Outdoor Outfitters to pursue a more aviation-focused career path. I had become increasingly interested in aircraft and airport operations after spending time watching planes at Merrill Field on my days off, and I realized I wanted to move into a field with more technical and long-term growth potential. When an entry-level line service position opened up at Anchorage Airfield Services, I decided to make the transition from retail to aviation, even though it meant changing industries and learning a completely new set of skills.
